text: The surgeon begins the procedure by decompressing the stomach using an oral gastric tube, which is inserted to reduce gastric volume.
The surgeon operates on the decompressed stomach, using a vessel sealer device held in the right hand to manage the gastric tissue.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer device to simultaneously seal blood vessels and cut tissue, facilitating rapid and bloodless dissection during the robotic sleeve gastrectomy.

The surgeon lifts the stomach, revealing surrounding adipose tissue, and begins dividing it while approaching the distal aspect of the stomach near the pylorus, the muscular sphincter at the stomach's end.
The surgeon maneuvers the robotic instruments to dissect along the body of the stomach, moving towards the right side of the screen and approaching the spleen.
The surgeon has accessed the lesser sac, the posterior space behind the stomach, and is lifting the stomach with the left hand to create a window for enhanced visualization.

The surgeon navigates up the greater curvature of the stomach, with the diaphragm visible above, showing the heart beating through it. A liver retractor is positioned to enhance visualization. The phrenic artery and vein are seen above the vessel sealer, supplying blood to the diaphragm.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the area where the esophagus meets the stomach, with the spleen coming into view as they proceed along the proximal part of the greater curve of the stomach. The vessel sealer is used for precise dissection near the spleen, operating within a centimeter to avoid injury and potential bleeding.

The surgeon continues to mobilize the posterior aspect of the stomach, carefully dissecting flimsy adhesions from the surrounding fat, spleen, and diaphragm. The goal is to achieve complete mobilization of this area.

The surgeon works near the diaphragm, examining the area where the esophagus meets the stomach. The view from above is used to check for a hiatal hernia, which would show a portion of the stomach protruding into the chest. The diaphragmatic crura are visible, which normally hold the stomach within the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the stomach tissue near the pylorus, ensuring preservation of this portion as it will remain with the patient post-surgery. The focus is on avoiding thermal injury to the pylorus while working towards it.

The surgeon introduces a stapler into the surgical field, preparing to pass a 36 French bougie with a blunt tip. This instrument is used to gauge the size of the sleeve during the gastrectomy procedure, ensuring consistency in the sleeve formation.
The bougie is passed by the anesthesiologist at the head of the patient, while Dr. Harnish controls the stapler, preparing for the sleeve gastrectomy.

The surgeon uses a specialized stapler with a black load, measuring sixty millimeters in length, to transect the stomach. Reinforced staple loads are applied to minimize bleeding during the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon removes the stapler after the first fire and reloads it, preparing to continue stapling along the bougie, which is visible within the body of the stomach.
The surgeon prepares for the second fire of the stapler using a purple load, following the initial black load, to continue transecting the stomach along the bougie.
The surgeon highlights the incisura of the stomach, emphasizing the importance of avoiding narrowing in this area during the sleeve gastrectomy procedure.
The stapler is removed and reloaded with a purple, sixty millimeter reinforced staple load, preparing for the next transection along the stomach.

The surgeon continues stapling along the body of the stomach using a 60mm purple staple load. The pancreas is visible posterior to the stomach, distinguishable from the surrounding fat, and care is taken to avoid it during the stapling process.
The surgeon performs the final stapler fires to remove the fundus of the stomach, carefully excising this portion which is significant for hormone secretion related to appetite.

The surgeon uses a 45-millimeter stapler to complete the transection of the stomach, having removed approximately 85% of the stomach over a 36 French bougie. The divided portion of the stomach is prepared for removal from the patient, concluding the operation after an EGD with a negative leak test.
